title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
principle: Make every state-changing request prove it came from your own UI. Use the framework's CSRF protection: a per-session token echoed on each unsafe request, or a double-submit cookie. Set session cookies SameSite=Lax or Strict, and never perform a state change on a GET. Don't hand-roll this; enable the middleware the framework ships.

snippets.secure:
# Flask
from flask_wtf import CSRFProtect
csrf = CSRFProtect(app)  # now every POST needs a valid token; {{ csrf_token() }} in the form
# Django: keep 'django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware' enabled and use {% csrf_token %}
snippets.note: Don't decorate real endpoints with @csrf.exempt / @csrf_exempt. Set SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = 'Lax'.

snippets.library: csrf-csrf / a double-submit token, plus SameSite cookies
snippets.note: The old 'csurf' package is deprecated; use csrf-csrf or your framework's built-in. SameSite alone is defense in depth, not a full substitute for a token.

snippets.vulnerable: http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()); // do not disable it
snippets.secure:
http.csrf(Customizer.withDefaults());
// Spring injects and validates the token; include it via Thymeleaf's automatic hidden field or the CookieCsrfTokenRepository for SPAs.
snippets.note: For a stateless JWT API that no browser calls with cookies, CSRF may not apply — but if you authenticate with cookies, keep it on.
gorilla/csrf validates the token on POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE automatically. Serve the token in the form or an X-CSRF-Token header for fetch().
